coredata.dc:title: Accurate detection and delineation boundary of renal cell carcinoma based on dual-targeted magnetic-fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Nana
Huang, Tonghui
Duan, Tengfei
Bao, Yu
Gao, Ruochen
Wang, Xiuzhi
Xu, Kai
Han, Cuiping
coredata.dc:description: Accurate tumor localization and tumor boundary delineation are significant to improve the effectiveness of cancer therapeutics. In the present study, a fluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) nanoprobe AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs with dual targeting function was developed for precise detection and delineation boundary of renal cell carcinoma (RCC). The dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe was initially established by co-conjugating peptide CLT1 and aptamer AS1411 to magnetic-fluorescent gadolinium(III)-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs). The proposed AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs with wide-range multi-color fluorescence emission could target-specific image 786-O RCC cells through three distinct fluorescence channels and achieved the quantitative determination of 786-O cells with a low detection limit of 552 cells mL−1. Besides, the dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe could accurately delineate tumor boundary and generate greater and prolonged plateau contrast enhancement than the non-targeted and single-targeted Gd-CDs. Furthermore, the small-sized nanoprobe could be easily excreted from the body by the renal excretory system after imaging without any obvious long-term toxicity. These studies demonstrated that the proposed dual-targeted nanoprobe appeared as a very promising fluorescence/MR imaging agent for accurate identification and localization of renal cancer tumors, which could be beneficial to the early diagnosis and treatment of RCC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-targeting
Tumor diagnosis
Renal cell carcinoma
Fluorescence imaging
Magnetic resonance imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AS1411 aptamer and CLT1 peptide conjugated Gd-CDs
Cellular targeting fluorescence imaging and detection
In vivo and ex vivo fluorescence imaging
Cell targeted fluorescence assay and cellular uptake
In vivo MR and fluorescence imaging
In vivo biocompatibility and toxicity analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical TEM image and high-resolution TEM image (insert) of as-synthesized Gd-CDs. (B) Full-scan XPS spectrum of Gd-CDs. (C) Gd4d spectrum. (D) Hydrodynamic particle size distribution of Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs. (E) UV–vis spectra of AS1411, CLT1, Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs. (F) Fluorescence spectra of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. The insert shows photographs of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs solution under natural light and multi-color emission under different excitation wavelengths of 360–380 nm (blue), 450–470 nm (green), and 565–575 nm (red), respectively.

(A) Multi-color CLSM imaging of cells. 786-O cells treated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs (a), AS1411-Gd-CDs (b), CLT1-Gd-CDs (c), and Gd-CDs (d) for 2 h, respectively. (e) HK-2 cells treated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for 2 h. Scale bar = 20 μm. (B) Quantitative analysis of cellular uptake of different nanoparticles in cells. (C) Bio-TEM images of 786-O cells and HK-2 cells treated with Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for 2 h, respectively. Scale bar = 2 μm.
CLSM imaging of HK-2 cells and 786-O cells treated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for 0.5 h, 1 h, and 2 h, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra of 786-O cells (A) and HK-2 cells (B) after incubated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for different times. The concentration of both cell lines was 2.50 × 106 cells mL−1. (C) Correlation of fluorescent signal intensity of 786-O and HK-2 cells at 415 nm after incubated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for different times. Fluorescence spectra of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs upon analyzing different concentrations of 786-O cells (D) and HK-2 cells (E). (F) The linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity at 415 nm and the concentrations of 786-O cells and HK-2 cells. The fluorescence intensities of the samples were detected on a fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

MR contrast ability of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs. (A) The T1-weighted images and pseudo-color images of Gd-DTPA, Gd-CDs, and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs at different concentrations. (B) Linear correlation between longitudinal relaxation rate (1/T1) and Gd concentration. T1-weighted MR images (C) and MR signal intensity (D) of 786-O and HK-2 cells after treated with Gd-CDs, CLT1-Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs, and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs (Probes), respectively. T1-weighted MR images (E) and the corresponding T1 signal intensity (F) of 786-O cells treated with different concentrations of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs (Probes), Gd-CDs, and Gd-DTPA.

T1-weighted MR imaging (up) and corresponding pseudo-color images (down) of RCC xenograft mice acquired at different times after intravenous administration of dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs (A), non-targeted Gd-CDs (B), and single-targeted AS1411-Gd-CDs and CLT1-Gd-CDs (C). The red arrowheads indicate tumors. (C) T1 signal intensity changes in the tumors entity (a) and tumor periphery (b) over time. (D) T1-weighted MR images of RCC xenograft mice pre- and post-injection of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs. The white arrows indicate the urinary bladder. (E) The contrast enhancement ratios of the tumors entity (a) and tumor periphery (b) over time, ΔSI = 100%×(SIpost-injection-SIpre-injection)/SIpre-injection.

In vivo fluorescence imaging performance of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs against RCC xenograft Balb/c mice. (A) In vivo fluorescence imaging of RCC xenograft Balb/c mice treated with Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs pre-injection and 3 h post-injection. The red circles indicate tumors. (B) Ex vivo fluorescence imaging of tumors and major organs harvested from RCC xenograft Balb/c mice 3 h after injection. (C) The averaged fluorescent intensity of major organs in ex vivo fluorescence imaging after 3 h post-injection of Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs.

Histological analysis of tumor tissue harvested from 786-O tumor baring Balb/c mice 3 h after injection of Gd-CDs (A) and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs (B). (a) Immunohistochemical staining of slices with anti-CD31 for stroma fibrinogen. (b) Immunohistochemical staining of slices with anti-CK7 for tumor cells. (c) hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of slices. (d–f) Multi-color CLSM imaging of 786-O tumor tissue (scale bar = 100 μm).

(A) Serum biochemistry analysis results obtained frommice before (0 days, control) and at 1, 7 and 21 days post intravenous injection of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs, and Gd-DTPA, respectively. (B) Histological images of the heart, liver, spleen lung, and kidneys of mice 1, 7 and 21 days post-intravenous injection of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs and in control mice. Scale bar = 100 μm.
Schematic illustration of the preparing procedure AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe and its application for dual-targeted fluorescence/MR imaging of RCC.

head.ce:title: Accurate detection and delineation boundary of renal cell carcinoma based on dual-targeted magnetic-fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China(Kai Xu, Cuiping Han).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate tumor localization and tumor boundary delineation are significant to improve the effectiveness of cancer therapeutics. In the present study, a fluorescence/magnetic resonance (MR) nanoprobe AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs with dual targeting function was developed for precise detection and delineation boundary of renal cell carcinoma (RCC). The dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe was initially established by co-conjugating peptide CLT1 and aptamer AS1411 to magnetic-fluorescent gadolinium(III)-doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs). The proposed AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs with wide-range multi-color fluorescence emission could target-specific image 786-O RCC cells through three distinct fluorescence channels and achieved the quantitative determination of 786-O cells with a low detection limit of 552 cells mL−1. Besides, the dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe could accurately delineate tumor boundary and generate greater and prolonged plateau contrast enhancement than the non-targeted and single-targeted Gd-CDs. Furthermore, the small-sized nanoprobe could be easily excreted from the body by the renal excretory system after imaging without any obvious long-term toxicity. These studies demonstrated that the proposed dual-targeted nanoprobe appeared as a very promising fluorescence/MR imaging agent for accurate identification and localization of renal cancer tumors, which could be beneficial to the early diagnosis and treatment of RCC.

ce:section.ce:para: Kidney cancer is a major public health issue worldwide and ranks as one of the leading top ten causes of cancer death [1]. Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the main pathological type and accounts for greater than 90% of neoplasms arising from the kidney [2]. RCC at an early stage is curable by surgery with little risk of recurrence, but 5-year survival was less than 20% once a metastatic disease developed [3]. Therefore, early screening and detection of RCC are crucial to improving the treatment efficacy of RCC. For kidney cancer that has not spread to distant sites, surgery is usually the initial treatment [4]. It's well known that the extent of tumor resection is positively correlated with clinical outcomes, as residual tumor margins are considered to be the main cause of recurrence. On the other hand, the surgical resection area cannot be extended too broadly to maintain normal renal functions. Therefore, precisely delineating the invasive margin for the complete removal of neoplastic tissue is of vital importance in RCC management. To address this issue, the design and development of safe target-specific imaging probes for effective early diagnosis of RCC and precise delineation of tumor boundaries are urgently required to improve the therapeutic efficacy and the life expectancy of the patients.

MR imaging is currently the most widely applied imaging technique for preoperative visualization of RCC because of its high spatial resolution in soft tissue [5]. Stable Gd(III) chelates (i.e., Gd-DTPA, Gd-DTOA) are currently the most widely applied contrast agents for clinical MR imaging to enhance imaging contrast by altering the surrounding water proton relaxation rate [6]. However, contrast-enhanced MR imaging modality is not effective for imaging and detection of biomarkers due to its low sensitivity and insufficient signal enhancement. Fluorescence/MR dual-modal imaging technology, combining the merits of high spatial resolution and deep tissue penetration of MR imaging with the high sensitivity of fluorescence, has become an important approach to overcome the limitation of single imaging modality to achieve more effective and sensitive diagnostic of tumor [7,8]. Fluorescence/MR dual-modal imaging methodology relies greatly on biocompatible, highly specific and sensitive imaging probes [9–11]. Carbon nanoparticles, especially fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), have emerged as attractive preferences to construct imaging contrast agents owning to their favorite properties including excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, nontoxicity, and facile preparation [12–14]. The doping of paramagnetic ions (Mn2+, Gd3+) into CDs is a possible route for the construction of magnetic-fluorescent CDs [15–18]. Du and co-workers prepared Gd3+-doped CDs as passive tumor-targeted fluorescence/MR imaging agents with an r1 relativity of 6.45 mM-1s−1 [19]. Yu et al. [20] have successfully used the Gd3+-doped CDs as theranostic agents for gene delivery and fluorescence/MR imaging of breast tumors. In our recent work, magnetic-fluorescent Gd3+/Mn2+-doped CDs were prepared successfully for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence imaging [21–23]. The previous promising results revealed that the prepared magnetic-fluorescent CDs had a more significant contrast enhancement than commercialized contrast agents in MR imaging, and their relatively small size (less than 10 nm) allowed efficient excretion via renal filtration. Therefore, the magnetic-fluorescent CDs conjugated with relevant biomarkers that are specifically associated with tumor aggressiveness and metastasis will provide an effective approach towards the early diagnosis of cancer [24,25]. However, to the best of our knowledge, the use of magnetic-fluorescent CDs for RCC-targeted fluorescence/MR imaging in vivo is virtually unexplored.

Various ligands specific to cancer-related biomarkers have been largely exploited to construct targeted vehicles for specific and effective delivery of imaging agents or therapeutic drugs to the tumor [26,27]. Among the numerous targeted agents, aptamer with chemical stability, easy synthesis and no immunogenicity has garnered a lot of attention [28]. AS1411 aptamer is a guanine-rich oligonucleotide that could specifically bind to the nucleolin, which is aberrantly upregulated in many types of tumor cells including RCC cells, both intracellularly and on the cell surface [29]. The AS1411-targeted MR and fluorescence/MR nanoprobes for improved detection of RCC have been reported [30,31].[30,31] However, it is critical to note that thus far few contrast agents could efficiently accumulate at the tumor periphery and delineate tumor boundary. It is well known that the tumor microenvironment closely correlates with tumor progression and metastasis [32]. The extracellular matrix is the main component of the tumor microenvironment, mainly composed of proteoglycan, collagen, laminin, fibronectin, and other components [33]. CLT1 is a cyclic peptide that specifically binds to the fibrin-fibronectin complexes in the extracellular matrix of tumors but rarely binds to normal tissues [34,35]. Hence, the dual-targeted molecular imaging probes combined the function of AS1411 aptamer targeting nucleolin overexpressed in tumor cells with the function of CLT1 peptide targeting fibrin-fibronectin complexes prevailing in the tumor stroma could effectively enable more precise visualization and diagnosis of RCC.

In this work, we designed and fabricated a dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe directed at both nucleolin and fibrin-fibronectin complexes for efficient targeting and fluorescence/MR dual-modal imaging of RCC (Scheme 1 ). The magnetic-fluorescent Gd-CDs were synthesized by a simple one-step solvent-free method using Magnevist (Gd-DTPA) and L-arginine (L-Arg) as precursors. Then, two types of targeting moieties, AS1411 aptamer and CLT1 peptide, were covalently linked onto the surface of the Gd-CDs via a carbodiimide technique. The ability of the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe for the targeted fluorescence/MR imaging of RCC was evaluated by comparison with non-targeted and single-targeted Gd-CDs using in vitro and in vivo assays. The biosafety of the nanoprobes was investigated by the serum biochemistry assay and histological analysis.

Magnevist (Gd-DTPA) was obtained from Bayer Schering Pharma AG (Berlin, Germany). 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). L-Arg and acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Company. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Carboxy-modified AS1411 aptamer (COOH-AS1411, 5′–COOH-TTTTTTGGTGGTGGTGGTTGTGGTGGTGGTGG-3′, ≧98%) was synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co.Ltd (Shanghai, China). CLT1 peptide (Cys-Gly-Gly-Gly-Cys(Acm)-Gly-Leu-Ile-Ile-Gln-Lys-Asn-Glu-Cys(Acm), ≧98%) was purchased from the Chinese Peptide Company (Hangzhou, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) (FEI Tecai G2F20, USA) were used to analyze the morphology and elemental composition of the prepared nanoparticles. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo escalab250Xi, USA) was used to determine the elemental composition. A dynamic light scattering analyzer (DSL, Zetasizer Nano ZS90) was used to measure the zeta potential and size distribution of nanoparticles. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded by a UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (UH4150, HITACH) and LS-45/55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Company, America), respectively. Cellular fluorescence images were obtained from laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM, Zeiss DMi3000B, Germany). Ultrathin sections of the cells and tissues were cut with Leica ultramicrotome (EM UC7, Germany). TEM images of cell sections were acquired from a Bio-TEM (Tecnai G2 Spirit BioTwin, FEI Company). A 3.0 T Discovery 750 W magnetic resonance system (GE Signa, USA) was used to get T1-weighted and T1-map images.

Gd-CDs were prepared by a simple solvent-free method according to the previous work [22]. Typically, a solution containing Gd-DTPA (100 μL, 469.01 mg mL−1) and L-Arg (0.1 mmol) was heated at 200 °C for 90 min. After cooling down, the solid product was dissolved in water and precipitated with three volumes of acetone. The resulting brown precipitate was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 10 min and then freeze-dried to acquire pure Gd-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AS1411 aptamer and CLT1 peptide conjugated Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: AS1411 aptamer and CLT1 peptide were covalently linked to the Gd-CDs surface using a carbodiimide technique. Briefly, COOH-AS1411 aptamer (50 μL, 100 μM) and CLT1 peptide (50 μL, 100 μM) were first activated by the mixture of EDC (5 mg) and NHS (3 mg) for 15 min (incubation at 37°C on shakers), respectively. Then, the activated AS1411 aptamer and CLT1 (50 μL, 100 μM) were added into Gd-CDs (500 μL, 50 mg mL−1) and reacted at 37 °C for 2 h. Similarly, single targeted AS1411-Gd-CDs and CLT1-Gd-CDs were obtained. The conjugated Gd-CDs were centrifuged and washed with PBS to remove the uncombined materials. The precipitate was redispersed into 500 μL of PBS and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular targeting fluorescence imaging and detection
ce:section.ce:para: A human normal liver cell line (HK-2) and human RCC line (786-O) were seeded into a 6-well cell culture plate and cultured in a complete medium for 24 h. Then the cells were incubated with a medium containing AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for another 2 h. Subsequently, the treated cells were washed three times, and fluorescence images of the cells were recorded on an LSCM. To quantify the fluorescence intensity of cells, the treated cells were harvested by centrifugation and redispersed in 200 μL PBS buffer. The fluorescence intensity of each example was measured using an LS-45/55 fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo and ex vivo fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The subcutaneous RCC-xenografted tumor models were established on four-week-old BALB/c mice. The RCC-xenografted mice were injected with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs (the dose of Gd was 5 mg kg−1) via tail vein and then subject to in vivo fluorescence imaging before and 3 h after the injection. Three hours post-administration, the mice were sacrificed by cervical dislocation to harvest major organs and tumors. The fluorescence images of these organs and tumors were acquired with the in vivo imaging system. For the histopathologicanalysis and immunohistochemistry imaging, the tumor tissues were harvested and cryosectioned into 5-µm slices immediately for fluorescence imaging, immunohistochemical analysis, and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)-stained. The ROI analysis was measured with the assistance of ImageJ software. The antibodies used in the immunohistochemical analysis are anti-CD31 (for stroma fibrinogen of renal clear cell carcinoma) and anti-CK7 (for the parenchyma of renal clear cell carcinoma). Animal experiments involved in this study were treated following the guidelines of the National Institutes of Health and approved by the ethics committee of Xuzhou Medical University.

To evaluate the ability of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs as a targeted MR imaging probe, the HK-2 cells and 786-O cells were separately incubated with Gd-CDs or AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs at a Gd dose of 1.34 mM for 2 h. Then, the cells were collected by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 5 min. After washing three times with PBS, the T1-weighted enhanced MR images and pseudo-color images were performed on an MR system with the following parameters: TE: Min Full, TR: 425 ms, spacing: 1.5 mm, Slice thickness: 3.0 mm, Field of View: 18 cm × 18 cm, Matrix size: 384 × 224. The Signal intensity was measured utilizing regions of interest (ROI) overlying cell pellets on T1-weighted original images.

The 786-O tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs at Gd concentration of 5 mg kg−1 to evaluate the targeted MR imaging, while the control groups of 786-O tumor-bearing mice were intravenously injected with Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs, and CLT1-Gd-CDs at the same dose. T1-weighted MR images and pseudo-color images were conducted pre- and post-injection. The sequences are as follows: TE: 3.2 ms, TR: 6 ms, FOV: 80 mm × 80 mm, slice thickness: 1 mm, slicing: 0.5 mm, Matrix size: 256 × 256. The signal values of tumors in the same ROI were selected to investigate the change of signal intensity at different time points.

Gd-CDs were prepared via a simple solvent-free method using Gd-DTPA and L-Arg as precursors. Previous work revealed that the carbonization temperature and reaction time has a great impact on the carbonization degree of the reactants in the formation of Gd-CDs, which would affect MR and fluorescent performance of the prepared Gd-CDs [36]. In the present work, a detailed study of the optimization of the reaction temperature and reaction time was conducted (Table S1). When the reaction was conducted at a temperature of 200 °C for 1.5 h, the resulting Gd-CDs showed superior MR and fluorescence imaging performance. The Gd-CDs prepared under this reaction condition were then selected for further experiments.

The TEM image analysis of Gd-CDs indicated excellent monodispersity and uniform spherical morphology (Fig. 1 A), and the high-resolution examination of the TEM image shows their crystalline nature with well-resolved lattice fringes (Fig. 1A insert). The EDXS analysis showed the Gd element doped in the nanoparticles (Fig. S1). The composition and the surface functional state of the prepared Gd-CDs were analyzed with XPS spectra and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The results showed that Gd-CDs were mainly composed of C, N, O and Gd elements (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution spectrum of Gd4d showed two typical binding energies at 146.9 and 141.7 eV, assigned to Gd 4d3/2 and Gd 4d5/2 (Fig. 1C), suggesting that the chemical environment after the formation of Gd-CDs was not significantly changed in part of Gd3+ [37]. Besides, the typical peaks at 284, 399 and 530 eV, corresponded with the binding energies of C1s, N1S and O1S, respectively. Moreover, the O1s spectra found peaks at 531.4 eV and 530.2 eV, which approved the presence of C-O and C = O bonds (Fig. S2A). Three peaks at 284.6, 285.7 and 287.9 eV in the C1s spectra revealed the existence of C–C, C-N and C-O, C = O bond, respectively (Fig. S2B). FTIR spectra (Fig. S3) further revealed that there were various amino, carboxylate, and hydroxyl functional groups located at the surface, which endowed the Gd-CDs with good hydrophilicity and facilitate bioconjugation with target ligands.

To increase the tumor target and cell specificity, AS1411 aptamer and CLT1 peptide were coupled to the surface of Gd-CDs via a carbodiimide (EDC/NHS) chemistry. Dynamic light scattering measurement revealed a 2.78 nm increase in the hydrodynamic particle size of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs compared with the Gd-CDs (from 4.85 nm to 7.63 nm) (Fig. 1D). The increment in the hydrodynamic size upon conjugation indicated that the successful conjugation reaction. The UV–vis spectra were used for further understanding the optical properties of the prepared nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 1E, Gd-CDs complexes showed a broad absorbance band at ∼ 330 nm. After the coupling, the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs exhibited two supernumerary peaks approximately at 227 nm and 253 nm corresponding to CLT1 and AS1411, respectively. All the results indicated the successful conjugation of CLT1 and AS1411 with Gd-CDs.

The emissioncharacteristicsof Gd-CDs were not significantly altered after chemical conjugation of CLT1 and AS1411 (Fig. S4). The resulting AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs aqueous solution was transparent and pale yellow in daylight but emitted bright blue, green, and red under different wavelength excitation lights (Fig. 1F insert). A detailed spectroscopy study revealed an excitation-dependent emission behavior of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1F, the maximum emission peak gradually red-shifted from 410 to 654 nm with increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 580 nm, indicating the probe possessed excellent multi-color fluorescent properties for imaging applications. The performance of multi-color fluorescence emission of the probe would eliminate external exciter dependency in bioimaging and biodiagnostics. Notably, the synthesized nanoprobe exhibited high stability in PBS buffer solution. The fluorescence intensity and T1 signal intensity remained unchanged after two weeks storage at 4 °C (Fig. S5). Free Gd3+ leached out from the probe wasnotdetectable by ICP-MS. The high stability of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs makes them suitable for using as a bioimaging probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell targeted fluorescence assay and cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficacy of our designed nanoprobe for targeted RCC cellular imaging, 786-O cells and HK-2 cells were incubated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs, CLT1-Gd-CDs, and Gd-CDs with the same Gd concentration for 2 h, then fluorescence images of the treated cells were observed under LSCM. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the 786-O cells treated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs showed strong blue, green, and red multi-color fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths, while bare Gd-CDs treated cells exhibited pale signal at the same conditions. Qualitative and quantitative analysis confirmed that the immobilized AS1411 on the Gd-CDs surface greatly enhanced cellular uptake (Fig. 2B), highly probably by AS1411-mediated endocytosis [38]. CLT1 modification also displayed an increased cellular uptake as compared to the bare Gd-CDs-treated groups, although to a lesser extent than AS1411. This could be explained by the positively charged CLT1-Gd-CDs (+14.3 mV) in physiological conditions, thereby adhering to the negatively charged surface of 786-O cells. On the contrary, the negative controls of the HK-2 cells exhibited very weak and randomly distributed fluorescence after the co-incubation with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs, which certified that the designed probe has high selectivity and binding affinity of the tumor cells.

Bio-TEM analysis was conducted to further compare the cellular uptake and intracellular distribution of nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 2C, the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe was internalized more than the bare Gd-CDs in 786-O cells at the same time. A majority of the nanoprobes were internalized into 786-O tumor cells and enters the nucleus efficiently. On the other hand, only a few particles were observed in the HK-2 cells, and there was no apparent difference between the Gd-CDs group and the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs group. The Bio-TEM results further confirmed that AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs could serve as targeted and efficient intracellular imaging probes.

The high specificity of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe to 786-O tumor cells enables them to be used as potential biosensors for 786-O cells detection. To evaluate the sensitivity of the probe to 786-O cells, the variation in fluorescence intensity with cell concentration was tested. As illustrated in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence intensity was intensified with the increased concentration of 786-O cells from 0 to 3.09 × 106 cells mL−1. The signal intensity at 415 nm was linear to the concentration of cells and resulted in the linear regression of F = 0.2443C + 95.886 (R2 = 0.9985) with a low detection limit of 552 cells mL−1 based on 3σ criteria (Fig. 4F). On the contrary, only a slight change in the fluorescence intensity was observed when the concentrations of HK-2 normal cells ranged from 0 to 2.89 × 106 cells mL−1 (Fig. 4E). These findings demonstrated that the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs afford selectivity and sensitivity to 786-O cells.

The presence of unpaired electrons in the outermost shell of Gd3+ created a strong local proton environment and contributed to the excellent contrast effect in T1-weighted images [25]. Doping Gd3+ into CDs endows them with T1-weighted MR imaging modality [39,40]. In addition, the much higher hydrodynamic radius and surface area of Gd-CDs compared to Gd-DTPA molecules allowed more Gd ions to access water molecules. This will help to shorten the longitudinal relaxation and result in better MRI properties than Gd-DTPA [19]. MR contrast ability of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs was performed on a 3.0 T clinical magnetic resonance scanner. For comparative purposes, the commercial contrast agent Gd-DTPA and bare Gd-CDs were also analyzed at the same Gd concentration. The T1-weighted images and pseudo-color images showed that the T1 signal intensity of the agents aggrandized gradually with the increasing Gd concentration (Fig. 5 A). It is noted that the longitudinal relaxation time of Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs was shortened than Gd-DTPA. According to the linear relationship between Gd concentrations and longitudinal relaxation time (Fig. 5B), the longitudinal relaxivities (r1) of Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs were measured to be 6.27 mM−1 s−1 and 6.46 mM−1 s−1, respectively, which was 1.43-fold and 1.47-fold in comparison with the Gd-DTPA (4.40 mM−1 s−1), indicating that the Gd-CDs nanoprobe has an excellent enhanced MR contrast ability.

To investigate MR imaging performance of the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for cancer cells detection, 786-O and HK-2 cells were incubated with Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs, CLT1-Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs under the same conditions, and subsequently examined using a T1-weight MRI. Fig. 5C clearly shows bright image contrast enhancement of the 786-O cells after incubation with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs compared with Gd-CDs and CLT1-Gd-CDs treated groups. For HK-2 cells, the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs conjugation did not induce an obvious signal enhancement compared with bare Gd-CDs, owing to the low expression of nucleolin in HK-2 normal cells [41]. The MR signal intensity value of 786-O cells treated with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs was 2.08-fold compared with the value of HK-2 group and 1.43-fold compared with the value of 786-O cells treated with Gd-CDs (Fig. 5D). It suggested that the AS1411 aptamer may favor the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs to be internalized by the nucleolin over-expressed in cancer cells, resulting in a great signal enhancement.

To further appraise and quantify the effects of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs at different Gd concentrations on cell-targeted MRI, the 786-O cells treated with Gd-DTPA, Gd-CDs, and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs at Gd concentrations of 0.57, 1.34, and 2.73 mM for 2 h were further analyzed by the MR system. As shown in Fig. 5E and F, the MR signal intensity value was increased continually with the increase of nanoparticles concentration. AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs treated group represented stronger contrast enhancement and higher signal value in comparison with the groups treated with Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at the same Gd concentration, indicating the superiority of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs as a target MRI nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo MR and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs on targeted MR and fluorescence imaging of tumor tissue was evaluated in 786-O RCC xenografts mice. T1-weighted MR images were acquired sequentially before and after intravenous administration of the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs as the target, and Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs and CLT1-Gd-CDs as control groups at various time points. In terms of T1-weighted signal enhancement tendency, the imaging effects of non-targeted Gd-CDs and single-targeted AS1411-Gd-CDs and CLT1-Gd-CDs groups were similar. The T1 signal intensity of the tumor gradually increased along with time after intravenous injection and reached peak levels at 0.5 h. Then, the signal gradually reduced and returned to its baseline in 2 h (Fig. 6 B and C). The AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs-treated group showed different enhancement patterns with the three groups above. The T1 signal in the tumor entity was much stronger than the other three groups at the same post-injection time points and peaked at 3 h post-injection (Fig. 6A). For quantitative comparison, the signal increase ratio (ΔSI) in the tumor tissues was calculated as ΔI = 100%×(SIpost-injection-SIpre-injection)/SIpre-injection, where SI means signal intensity. The dual-targeted contrast agent resulted in a 57.1% signal increase ratio in the tumor, while the peak ΔSI values in the tumor were about 27.4%, 31.9%, and 26.2% increase for Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs, and CLT1-Gd-CDs groups, respectively (Fig. 6E). These results indicated that AS1411 and CLT1 co-conjugation could further accumulate nanoparticles at the tumor site, resulting in significant and prolonged tumor contrast enhancement. Remarkably, mice with AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs injection displayed a significantly highlighted boundary at the tumor periphery, which provided highly detailed edge imaging and precisely distinguished tumor tissue from normal tissues. At the different post-injection time points, the dual-targeted nanoprobes showed relatively stronger T1-weight signals at the tumor periphery compared to the other three groups. The ΔSI at the tumor periphery was increased to 206% at 3 h post-injection, which was an about 1.58-fold increase compared to CLT1-Gd-CDs and 1.8-fold than that of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs at 0.5 h post-injection. This significant signal enhancement was probably due to the presence of fibrin-fibronectin complexes specifically recognized by CLT1 peptides in the densely packed vessels containing the zones of stroma outside the edge of the tumor [42]. Notably, the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs probe maintained a high T1 signal in the tumor until 5 h after administration, showing its long-time retention in the tumor. The enhanced clear vision of tumor boundary at 10 min-5 h after injection provided a wide time window for MR imaging, suggesting excellent MR imaging capacity of the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs for tumor diagnosis.

Besides, high contrast enhancement in the urinary bladder was observed within 6 h post-injection of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs and the signal then disappeared 12 h after administration (Fig. 6D), indicating that the prepared nanoprobe with small particle size was eliminated from the body by the urinary system, which was consistent with previously reported results [43]. Size is an important factor that affects the renal clearance efficiency of particles. Typically, spherical nanoparticles with a hydrodynamic size less than 8 nm can be effectively cleared out from the body through the kidney [44]. The AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs particles with a hydrodynamic diameter of 7.63 nm could be effectively excreted out of the body and hence reduced the impact of long-term retention. The blood circulation half-life of nanoparticles tested by tracking the T1 signal in blood samples at different time points after intravenous administration was 250.3 min (Fig. S6), which was longer than that of Magnevist used in clinical applications (about 16.2 min) [45]. This moderate blood circulation time made it possible for AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs efficient accumulation in the tumors and also to be excreted from the body after imaging [46].

To further verify the targeted ability of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs, in vivo fluorescence imaging experiments were conducted. As presented in Fig. 7 A, the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs accumulation in tumor tissue after 3 h post-injection was much stronger than that of non-targeted Gd-CDs. In consistence with the in vivo imaging, the ex vivo fluorescence imaging of tumors harvested at 3 h showed that AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs displayed higher intensity than that of Gd-CDs in the tumor region (Fig. 7B and C). Besides, high fluorescence intensity was observed in the kidney, ureter and urinary bladder, but faint fluorescence in the liver, indicating the nanoprobes were mainly cleared from the body by the urinary system, which was consistent with the result of in vivo MR imaging.

To confirm the active uptake of nanomaterials by tumors from the pathological level, the frozen section, immunohistochemistry staining, and H&E-staining slice of tumors were respectively evaluated. A homogenous and abundant existence of tumor cells with obvious heterogenicity, large and hyperchromatic nuclei, and fibrin-fibronectin complexes in 786-O tumor slices were demonstrated by H&E-staining and immunohistochemistry staining (Fig. 8 ). Furthermore, AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs distributed more diffusely and deeply in tumor tissues than Gd-CDs, with a large amount of green, blue, and red fluorescence was observed in frozen sections, and the site was consistent with the immunohistochemistry staining. It was confirmed that the dual-targeted molecular probe AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs could specifically bind to the nucleolin and fibrin-fibronectin complexes, which are over-expressed on the tumor tissue in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility and toxicity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Biosafety of nano-theranostic agents is critical for the translation into clinical application. To evaluate the biocompatibility and potential toxicity of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs, serum biochemistry and histological analysis were performed. As shown in Fig. 9 A, both Gd-CDs and AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs revealed similar effects on serum biochemical parameters to the mice treated with the Gd-DTPA. The renal function markers, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CREA), and two crucial hepatic indicators, albumin (ALB) and total protein (TP), were all within normal levels for all the treated mice during the 21-day test period. Although the hepatic function indicators–alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) revealed slight variations after one day post-injection, they returned to the normal ranges in 7 and 21 days after administration. It demonstrated that the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs, and Gd-DTPA may affect hepatic function transiently after administration without long-term injury to the normal renal function and hepatic function. Moreover, the H&E stained tissue sections of organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) from mice 1, 7 and 21 days post-injection of AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs showed no histopathological abnormalities or lesions compared to the control group (Fig. 9B). The preliminary results indicated the low toxicity and favorable biocompatibility of the AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe in vivo, promoting its further potential applications in biomedical fields.

A novel dual-targeted AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe was developed for effective fluorescence/MR imaging of clear renal cell carcinoma. The proposed AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe can not only achieve specific multi-color fluorescence imaging and MR tracking of clear cell renal carcinoma cells but also improve accumulation in tumors and tumor boundary, generating more significant contrast enhancement in tumor and clearer visualization of tumor boundary. Importantly, aside from their good biocompatibility, the probes could also be efficiently excreted from the body via the urinary excretion system after imaging, avoiding the accumulation of probes in the body and the potential for long-term toxicity. The magnetic-fluorescent AS1411-CLT1-Gd-CDs nanoprobe shows great promise to precisely image and guide the resection of clear cell renal carcinoma, and even could additionally be modified for theranostic applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science of China (81771904, 81671744, and 82172005), Six Talent Peaks of Jiangsu Province (WSW-051), Science and Technology Planning Project of Xuzhou (KC21164 and KC20104), and Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents.
